verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the day following Paul went in with us unto James: and all the elders were present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he had saluted them, he declared particularly what things God had wrought among the Gentiles by his ministry.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was the Bishop of Jerusalem; and to him (Paul) is sent on an earlier occasion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This (James) was brother of the Lord; a great and admirable man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (To him, it says,) “Paul entered in with us.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the (Bishop’s) unassuming behavior: “and the elders” (were present).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again Paul relates to them the things relating to the Gentiles, not indulging in vainglory, God forbid, but wishing to show forth the mercy of God, and to fill them with great joy. (ch. xv.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See accordingly: “when they heard it,” it says, “they glorified God,”—not praised nor admired Paul: for in such wise had he narrated, as referring all to Him—“and said unto him, Thou seest, brother, how many thousands of Jews there are which believed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe with what modest deference they too speak: “they said to him:” not (James) as Bishop discourses authoritatively, but they take Paul as partner with them in their view; “Thou seest, brother:” as though immediately and at the outset apologizing for themselves, and saying, “We did not wish this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou the necessity of the thing? ‘how many thousands,’ say they, ‘of Jews there are which’ have come together.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they say not, “how many thousands we have made catechumens,” but, “there are.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And these,” say they, “are all zealous for the law.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Two reasons—the number of them, and their views.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For neither had they been few, would it have been right to despise them: nor, if they were many and did not all cling to the law, would there have been need to make much account of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then also a third cause is given: “And they all,” it says, “have been informed of thee”—they say not,“have heard,” but κατηχήθησαν, that is, so they have believed, and have been taught, “that thou teachest apostasy from Moses to all the Jews which are among the Gentiles, by telling them not to circumcise their children, neither to walk after the customs.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What is it therefore? the multitude must needs come together: for they will hear that thou art come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do therefore this that we say to thee” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22, 23): they say these things as advising, not as commanding.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “We have four men which have a vow on them; them take, and purify thyself with them, and be at charges with them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Make thy defence in act, not in word—“that they may shave themselves,” it says, “and all may know that those things, whereof they were informed concerning thee, are nothing; but that thou thyself also walkest orderly, and keepest the law” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23, 24): they say not, “teachest,” but, of superabundance, “that thou thyself also keepest the law.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of course not this was the matter of chief interest, whether he did not teach others, but, that he did himself observe the law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What then” (he might say), “if the Gentiles should learn it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I shall injure them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How so? say they, seeing that even we, the teachers of the Jews, have sent unto them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As touching the Gentiles which believe, we have written and concluded that they observe no such thing, save only that they keep themselves from things offered to idols, and from blood, and from strangled, and from fornication.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here with a kind of remonstrance (ἐντρεπτικὥς), As “we,” say they, commanded them, although we are preachers to the Jews, so do thou, although a preacher to the Gentiles, cooperate with us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe Paul: he does not say, “Well, but I can bring forward Timothy, whom I circumcised: well, but I can satisfy them by what I have to say (of myself):” but he complied, and did all: for in fact thus was it expedient (to do).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was one thing to take (effectual) measures for clearing himself, and another to have done these things without the knowledge of any (of the parties).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was a step open to no suspicion, the fact of his even bearing the expenses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Paul took the men, and the next day purifying himself with them entered into the temple, signifying the accomplishment of the days of purification, until that an offering should be offered for every one of them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Signifying,” διαγγέλλων, i.e. καταγγέλλων, publicly notifying: so that it was he who made himself conspicuous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when the seven days were about to be completed, the Jews from Asia”—for (his arrival) most keeps times with theirs—“when they saw him in the temple, stirred up all the people, and laid hands on him, crying out, Men of Israel, help: This is the man, that teacheth all men everywhere against the people, and the law, and this place: and further brought Greeks also into the temple, and hath polluted this holy place.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark their habitual conduct, how turbulent we everywhere find it, how men who with or without reason make a clamor in the midst.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For they had seen before with him in the city Trophimus an Ephesian, whom they supposed that Paul had brought into the temple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And all the city was moved, and the people ran together: and they took Paul, and drew him out of the temple and forthwith the doors were shut.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Men of Israel,” it says, “help: this is the man that (teaches) against the people, and the law, and this place.”—the things which most trouble them, the Temple and the Law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul does not tax the Apostles with being the cause of these things to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they drew him,” it says, “out of the Temple: and the doors were shut.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they wished to kill him; and therefore were dragging him out, to do this with greater security.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as they went about to kill him, tidings came unto the tribune of the cohort, that all Jerusalem was in an uproar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who immediately took soldiers and centurions, and ran down unto them: and when they saw the tribune and the soldiers, they left beating of Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then the tribune came near, and took him, and commanded him to be bound with two chains; and demanded who he was, and what he had done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And some cried one thing, some another, among the multitude.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the tribune having come down delivered him, and “commanded him to be bound with two chains:” (hereby) appeasing the anger of the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he could not know the certainty for the tumult, he commanded him to be carried into the castle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he came upon the stairs, so it was, that he was borne of the soldiers for the violence of the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the multitude of the people followed after, crying, Away with him!” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means, “Away with him?” that is, what they say with us according to the Roman custom, To the standards with him!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as Paul was to be led into the castle, he said unto the tribune, May I speak unto thee?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the act of being borne along up the stairs, he requests to say something to the tribune: and observe how quietly he does it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “May I speak unto thee?” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who said, Canst thou speak Greek?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Art thou not then that Egyptian, which before these days madest an uproar, and leddest out into the wilderness four thousand men that were murderers?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For (this Egyptian) was a revolutionary and seditious person.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With regard to this then Paul clears himself, and * *

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Do therefore this that we say unto thee,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows that it was not necessary to do this upon principle (προηγουμένως)—whence also they obtain his compliance—but that it was economy and condescension.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As touching the Gentiles,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, then, this was no hindrance to the preaching, seeing they themselves legislated for them to this effect.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, then, in his taking Peter to task he does not absolutely (ἁπλὥς) charge him with doing wrong: for precisely what he does on this occasion himself, the same does Peter on that occasion, (merely) holding his peace, and establishing his doctrine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he says not, For why? it is not right to teach those among the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “It is not enough to have not (so) preached there, but there was need also to do something more, that those may be persuaded that thou observest the law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The affair is one of condescension, be not alarmed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They do not advise him (to this course) sooner, until they have first spoken of the economy and the gain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And besides, the doing this in Jerusalem, is a thing to be borne.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: ‘Do thou this thing therefore’ here, that it may be in thy power abroad to do the other.” (b) “The next day,” it says, “he took them” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26): he deferred it not; for when there is economy in the case, this is the way of it. (a) “Jews from Asia having seen him,” for it was natural that they were spending some days there, “in the Temple.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27.) (c) Mark the economy (of Providence) that appeared (in this). (p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 279, note 1) After the (believing) Jews had been persuaded (concerning him), then it is that those (Jews of Asia) set upon him in order that those (believing Jews) may not also set upon him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Help, say they, “ye men of Israel!” as though it were some (monster) difficult to be caught, and hard to be overcome, that has fallen into their hands.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “All men,” they say, “everywhere, he ceaseth not to teach;” not here only.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And then the accusation (is) more aggravated by the present circumstances.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And yet more,” say they, “he has polluted the temple, having brought into it men who are Greeks.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet in Christ’s time there “came up (Greeks) to worship” (John xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20): true, but here it speaks of Greeks who had no mind to worship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they seized Paul,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They no longer wanted laws nor courts of justice: they also beat him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he forbore to make his defence then; he made it afterward: with reason; for they would not even have heard him then.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Pray, why did they cry, “Away with him?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They feared he might escape them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how submissively Paul speaks to the tribune.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “May I speak unto thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then art not thou that Egyptian?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This Egyptian, namely, was a cheat and impostor, and the devil expected to cast a cloud over (the Gospel) through him, and implicate both Christ and His Apostles in the charges pertaining to those (imposters): but he prevailed nothing, nay the truth became even more brilliant, being nothing defeated by the machinations of the devil, nay rather shining forth all the more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since if there had not been impostors, and then these (Christ and His Apostles) had prevailed, perhaps some one might have laid hold upon this: but when those impostors did actually appear, this is the wonder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In order,” says (the Apostle), “that they which are approved may be made manifest.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Gamaliel says, “Before these days stood up Theudas.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not grieve that heresies exist, seeing that false Christs wished to attack even Christ both before this and after; with a view to throw Him into the shade, but on every occasion we find the truth shining out transparent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So it was with the Prophets: there were false prophets, and by contrast with these they shone the more: just as disease enhances health, and darkness light, and tempest calm.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is no room left for the Greeks to say that (our teachers) were impostors and mountebanks: for those (that were such) were exposed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was the same in the case of Moses: God suffered the magicians, on purpose that Moses might not be suspected to be a magician: He let them teach all men to what length magic can go in making a fantastic show: beyond this point they deceived not, but themselves confessed their defeat.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Impostors do us no harm, rather do us good, if we will apply our mind to the matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then, you will say, if we are partners with them in common estimation?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The estimation is not among us, but with those who have no judgment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let not us greatly care for the estimation of the many, nor mind it more than needs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To God we live, not to men: in heaven we have our conversation, not on earth: there lie the awards and the prizes of our labors, thence we look for our praises, thence for our crowns.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus far let us trouble ourselves about men—that we do not give and afford them a handle against us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if, though we afford none, those choose to accuse us thoughtlessly and without discrimination, let us laugh, not weep.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Provide” thou “things honest before the Lord and before men” (2 Cor. viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 21): if, though thou provide things honest, that man derides, give thyself no more concern (for that).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou hast thy patterns in the Scriptures.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, saith he, “do I now persuade men or God?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10) and again, “We persuade men, but we are made manifest unto God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Christ (spoke) thus of them that take offence: “Let them alone, they be blind guides of the blind” (Matt. xv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14); and again, “Woe unto you, when all men speak well of you” (Luke vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26): and again, “Let your works shine, that men may see, and glorify your Father which is in heaven.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, “Whoso shall offend one of these little ones, it were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and he were drowned in the depths of the sea.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These sayings are not contrary, nay, they are exceedingly in accord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when the offence is with us, then woe unto us, but when not with us, not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again, Woe to (that man) through whom “the name of God is blasphemed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then if I do what is right in anything, but another blasphemes?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That is nothing to me, but only to him: for through him (God) was blasphemed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And how is it possible to do what is right in anything, and yet give a handle to the rest?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence will ye that I bring examples—from present, or from old times?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not to be easily scared (ψοφοδεεἵς), shall we speak to the very point now in hand?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul judaized in Jerusalem, but in Antioch not so: he judaized, and they were offended (p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 282, note 3), but those had no right to be offended.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is said to have saluted both Nero’s cupbearer and his concubine: what, think ye, must they have said against him because of this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they had no right to do so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since, if he drew them to him for loose living or any wicked acts, one might well be offended: but if in order to right living, what is there to be offended at?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let me mention something that happened to one of my acquaintance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The wrath of God once fell upon (a city), and he being very young (was) in the order of deacon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The bishop was absent at the time, and of the presbyters none took thought for the matter, but indiscriminately they caused in one night immense numbers of people to be baptized all at once, and they did indiscriminately receive baptism, all of them ignorant of everything: these he took apart by a hundred or two hundred together, and discoursed to them, not upon any other subject, but only on the sacraments, so that the unbaptized also were not allowed to be present.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many thought he did this because he coveted rule.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he cared not for that: neither however did he continue the thing for a (longer) time, but immediately desisted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was he the cause of the scandal?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I think not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if indeed he had done this without cause, they might with reason have ascribed it to him: and so again, if he had continued to do so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when aught of what is pleasing to God is hindered by another’s taking offence, it is right to take no notice: but then is the time to mind it, when we are not forced because of him to offend God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, say, if, while we are discoursing and putting drunkards to shame (σκωπτόντων), any one take offence—am I to give over speaking?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear Christ say, “Will ye also go away?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So then, the right thing is, neither to take no notice, nor to take too much, of the weakness of the many.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do we not see the physicians acting thus: how, when it may be done, they humor the whims of their patients, but when the gratification does harm, then they will not spare?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Always it is good to know the right mean.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many reviled, because a certain beautiful virgin stayed, and they railed upon those who catechised (her).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was it their duty to desist for that?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For let us not look to this only, whether some be offended, but whether they are justly offended, and so that it is no hurt to ourselves (to give way).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “If meat,” saith (Paul), “offend my brother, I will eat no meat as long as the world lasts.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Cor. viii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With reason: for the not eating did (him) no harm.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If however it offend him, that I wish to renounce (ἀποτάξασθαι) (the world), it is not right to mind him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And whom, you will ask, does this offend?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many, to my knowledge.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When therefore the hindrance is a thing indifferent, let (the thing) be done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Else, if we were to look only to this, many are the things we have to desist from: just as, on the other hand, if we should despise (all objections), we have to destroy many (brethren).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As in fact Paul also took thought beforehand concerning offence: “Lest,” he says, “in this liberality which is administered by us:” for it was attended with no loss (to him) to obviate an ill surmise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when we fall into such a necessity as that great evils should ensue through the other’s taking offence let us pay no heed to that person.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He has to thank himself for it, and we are not now accountable, for it was not possible to spare him without hurt (to ourselves).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Some were offended, because certain believers sat down to meat in (heathen) temples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not right to sit down: for no harm came of this (their not doing it).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They were offended, because Peter ate with the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he indeed spared them, but (Paul) not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On all occasions it behooves us in following the laws of God to take great pains that we give no matter of offence; that both ourselves may not have to answer for it, and may have mercy vouchsafed us from God, by the grace and loving-kindness of His only-begotten Son, with Whom to the Father and Holy Ghost together be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, world without end.
